section.title: Dead to Sin, Alive in Christ
section.sectionRationale: Having declared that where sin increased, grace abounded all the more, Paul now answers the obvious objection: shall we then keep on sinning so that grace may increase? His reply is an emphatic no, grounded not in willpower but in union with Christ. We died to sin; we were baptized into His death, buried with Him, and raised with Him to walk in newness of life. Our old self was crucified with Him, so we are to count ourselves dead to sin and alive to God, and refuse to let sin reign, for we are not under law but under grace.
section.thesis: Believers cannot go on living in sin, because in union with Christ they have died to it: joined to His death, burial, and resurrection, their old self was crucified with Him, so they must reckon themselves dead to sin and alive to God, and no longer let sin reign, since they stand not under law but under grace.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the objection answered, we died to sin; baptized into Christ's death and raised to newness of life; our old self crucified with Him; the great reckoning, count yourselves dead to sin and alive to God; and the summons, do not let sin reign, for you are under grace.

units.label: Shall we continue in sin?
units.note: by no means, we died to sin

verses.text: What then shall we say? Shall we continue in sin so that grace may increase?
Certainly not! How can we who died to {{hamartia|sin}} live in it any longer?

units.label: Baptized into His death, raised to new life
units.note: buried with Him, raised to walk in newness of life

verses.text: Or aren’t you aware that all of us who were {{baptizo|baptized}} into Christ Jesus were baptized into His death?
We were therefore {{sunthapto|buried with Him}} through baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, we too may walk in newness of life.
For if we have been united with Him like this in His death, we will certainly also be united with Him in His resurrection.

units.label: Our old self crucified with Him
units.note: the body of sin rendered powerless

verses.text: We know that our {{palaios_anthropos|old self}} was crucified with Him so that the body of sin might be rendered powerless, that we should no longer be slaves to sin.
For anyone who has died has been freed from sin.

units.label: Count yourselves dead to sin and alive to God

verses.text: Now if we died with Christ, we believe that we will also live with Him.
For we know that since Christ was raised from the dead, He cannot die again; death no longer has dominion over Him.
The death He died, He died to sin once for all; but the life He lives, He lives to God.
So you too must count yourselves dead to sin, but alive to God in Christ Jesus.

units.label: Do not let sin reign
units.note: you are not under law but under grace

verses.text: Therefore do not let sin reign in your mortal body so that you obey its desires.

Do not present the parts of your body to sin as instruments of wickedness, but present yourselves to God as those who have been brought from death to life; and present the parts of your body to Him as instruments of righteousness.
For sin shall not be your master, because you are not under law, but under {{charis|grace}}.

hamartia.gloss: Sin, a missing of the mark, understood by Paul not merely as separate acts but as a ruling power, almost personified, that enslaves and reigns over people. In Romans 6 sin is the tyrant whose dominion has been broken. To have died to sin is to have been severed from its reign and mastery, so that the believer no longer belongs to it or lives under its rule.

baptizo.gloss: To baptize, to immerse or plunge. Paul says we were baptized into Christ Jesus and into His death. Baptism is the sign and seal of union with Christ, marking the believer's incorporation into His death, burial, and resurrection. The outward washing points to the deeper reality: the believer has been joined to Christ so that His history becomes theirs.

sunthapto.gloss: To bury together with. Paul says we were buried with Christ through baptism into death. Burial confirms that a death has truly occurred; it is the seal on the grave. Believers are so united to Christ that His burial is theirs, the decisive end of the old life, so that they may rise with Him to walk in newness of life.

palaios_anthropos.gloss: The old self or old man, what we were in Adam, the whole person under sin's dominion and condemnation. Paul says this old self was crucified with Christ, put to death in union with Him, so that the body of sin might be rendered powerless. The believer is no longer that person; a decisive break with the old identity has already taken place at the cross.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's undeserved favor and saving power. To be under grace rather than under law is to live in a new realm and under a new King, where sin's dominion is broken. Far from licensing sin, grace is the very reason sin shall not be master, for grace reigns through righteousness to eternal life.

basic.context: Paul has just said that where sin increased, grace abounded all the more. That raises a sharp question: if grace grows greater the more we sin, why not sin freely? Paul answers with a resounding no. We died to sin, so how can we still live in it? He explains this through our union with Jesus, pictured in baptism: we were joined to His death, buried with Him, and raised with Him to a new kind of life.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the whole passage as one argument built on union with Christ. Paul reasons from what is already true of believers, we died and were raised with Christ, to how we must now live. The order matters: he does not say try hard to die to sin, but rather know that you have died to sin, then count it true, then live it out. Fact, faith, and then obedience.
basic.originalLanguages: The key words carry the argument. We died to hamartia, sin as a ruling power. We were baptizo, baptized, into Christ's death, and sunthapto, buried with Him. Our palaios anthropos, the old self we were in Adam, was crucified with Him. And now we live under charis, grace, not law, in a new realm where sin cannot reign.
basic.typology: The death and resurrection of Christ, into which the believer is joined, fulfills the pattern of the exodus, where God's people passed through the waters out of bondage into new life. Baptism echoes that passage through water from slavery to freedom. The old self crucified answers the old world drowned in the flood, and new life rising answers the new creation begun in Christ.
basic.christ: Everything here flows from Jesus. His death was a death to sin once for all, and His resurrection is a life lived to God, and both become the believer's own through union with Him. We are baptized into His death, buried with Him, raised with Him. Because He can never die again and death has no dominion over Him, the life we share with Him is secure. Christ is not merely our example but the one in whom we have died and risen.
basic.god: God is the one who raised Christ from the dead through His own glory, and who brings believers from death to life. It is to God that Christ lives, and to God that we are now alive in Christ Jesus. God reigns in grace over those who were once ruled by sin, and He calls them to present themselves to Him as instruments of righteousness.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 5 sets Adam against Christ, the two heads of humanity. Colossians 2 speaks of being buried and raised with Christ in baptism. Galatians 2 declares I have been crucified with Christ. Ephesians 2 says God made us alive together with Christ and raised us up with Him.
basic.oneStory: The Bible tells of humanity enslaved to sin and death since Adam. Here Paul announces the great turning: in Christ, believers have died to that old dominion and risen to new life. The story that ran from bondage in Egypt to the exodus, from exile to return, reaches its fulfillment as sinners are joined to the crucified and risen Lord and set free to walk in newness of life.

context.literary: Chapter 6 opens the next major movement of Romans. Having expounded justification by faith in chapters 3 to 5, Paul turns to the objection that grace might encourage sin, and answers it through union with Christ. The passage is structured by three great appeals to knowledge, do you not know, we know, we know, followed by the command to count and the command to present, moving from indicative to imperative.
context.historical: Paul writes to a mixed church in Rome that would have understood baptism as the public entry into the Christian community. Some critics of his gospel of free grace evidently charged that it undercut holy living, a charge he had already noted in chapter 3. Here he refutes it head on, showing that grace does not loosen the believer from holiness but grounds it.
context.canonical: Romans 6 is the classic New Testament text on union with Christ and the believer's death to sin. It undergirds the doctrine of sanctification, teaching that holiness flows from what God has already done in Christ. It stands with Colossians 2 and 3 and Ephesians 2 as a foundational statement of the believer's participation in Christ's death and resurrection.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To refute the charge that justification by grace encourages sin, and to show that believers, being united to Christ in His death and resurrection, have died to sin's dominion and been raised to new life, so that continuing in sin is a contradiction of who they now are in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul first describes what is objectively true of every believer, that they died and rose with Christ, then prescribes the response: count yourselves dead to sin and alive to God, and do not let sin reign. The indicative of union grounds the imperative of holiness; the commands rest on the accomplished fact.

debates.question: When and how did the believer die to sin?

views.label: A decisive past event in union with Christ
views.case: The historic Reformed reading takes the aorist tenses seriously: believers died to sin once, definitively, when they were united to Christ in His death, so sanctification begins from an accomplished break with sin's reign.

views.label: A gradual moral process to achieve
views.case: Some read it as an ongoing effort to put sin to death over time, stressing the ethical struggle; yet Paul's point is that the decisive death has already happened, and the struggle flows from that reality.

debates.bearing: Foregrounding the decisive break guards the gospel: holiness is not a self-improvement project but the outworking of a death and resurrection already accomplished in Christ, which the believer is called to reckon true and live out.

debates.question: What does baptized into Christ refer to?

views.label: Union with Christ, signified and sealed in baptism
views.case: The historic reading sees baptism as the God-given sign of the deeper reality of union with Christ by the Spirit through faith; the water points to and confirms incorporation into His death and resurrection.

views.label: The rite of water baptism itself as the effective cause
views.case: Some read it as though the ceremony automatically conveys the union; but Paul's emphasis falls on the spiritual reality the sign points to, received by faith, not on the rite working of itself.

debates.bearing: Keeping the sign and the thing signified together honors baptism while grounding assurance in Christ, so that the believer looks not to the ritual but to union with the crucified and risen Lord.

study.originalLanguages: The argument turns on a cluster of weighty words. Believers have died to hamartia, sin, which Paul treats as a reigning power, not merely as scattered wrongdoings. They were baptizo, baptized, into Christ Jesus and into His death, and sunthapto, buried together with Him, the grave sealing the end of the old life. Their palaios anthropos, the old self they were in Adam, was crucified with Christ so that the body of sin is rendered powerless. And they now live under charis, grace, a new realm and a new reign in which sin cannot be master, for grace reigns through righteousness unto life.

typology.fulfillment: Israel passing through the sea from slavery to freedom foreshadows baptism into Christ, the passage from bondage under sin to newness of life.
The believer's dying and rising with Christ fulfills the pattern of the seed that dies to bear fruit and the sacrifices that pictured death giving way to life.
What was pictured in the old world judged and a new creation begun is fulfilled as the old man in Adam is put to death and the believer walks in newness of life.

study.christ: Christ is the center of the whole passage. His death was a real death to sin, died once for all, and His risen life is a life lived to God, and by union with Him both become ours. We are baptized into His death, buried with Him, and raised with Him to walk in newness of life. Because He was raised never to die again, and death no longer has dominion over Him, the new life we share is secure and unbreakable. Paul does not merely hold up Christ as a model; he declares that the believer is so joined to Christ that His history is now the believer's own.
study.god: God the Father raised Christ from the dead by His own glory, and He is the one who brings sinners from death to life. Christ lives to God, and believers are now alive to God in Christ Jesus. God's grace reigns over those once ruled by sin, and He summons them to present themselves to Him and their bodies as instruments of righteousness. The passage exalts a God who not only forgives but liberates, breaking sin's dominion and claiming His people for holiness.

crossReferences.note: Adam and Christ as the two heads of humanity; in Christ grace reigns through righteousness to eternal life.
Buried with Christ in baptism and raised with Him through faith in the working of God.
I have been crucified with Christ, and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me.
God made us alive together with Christ and raised us up and seated us with Him.

study.oneStory: Since Adam, humanity has lived enslaved to sin and under the reign of death. The whole story of Scripture moves toward rescue: an exodus from bondage, a return from exile, a new creation. Here that rescue is applied to the believer through union with Christ. Joined to His death and resurrection, we have died to the old dominion of sin and risen to new life, no longer under law but under grace, walking as those brought from death to life.

quiz.q: How does Paul answer the objection, shall we continue in sin so that grace may increase?

options.text: Certainly not, because we died to sin and cannot live in it any longer.
options.note: Paul grounds his no in the believer's death to sin in union with Christ.

options.text: We may sin a little, since grace covers it anyway.
options.note: This is the very error Paul rejects; grace does not license sin.

options.text: We should sin so that grace can be displayed more fully.
options.note: Paul calls this unthinkable; it contradicts our death to sin.

options.text: Sin is no longer a real problem for believers.
options.note: There is real truth that sin's reign is broken, yet Paul still says do not let sin reign, so it must be resisted.

quiz.q: What does baptism into Christ signify, according to Paul?

options.text: Union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection.
options.note: We were baptized into His death, buried with Him, and raised to new life.

options.text: Only a public ceremony with no deeper meaning.
options.note: Baptism is indeed an outward sign, but it points to the deeper reality of union with Christ, so this understates its meaning.

options.text: A guarantee of salvation apart from faith in Christ.
options.note: The sign points to union with Christ received by faith, not the rite working of itself.

options.text: The moment a person earns God's favor by obedience.
options.note: Union with Christ is by grace, not earned by the act.

quiz.q: What happened to the old self, according to verse 6?

options.text: It was crucified with Christ so the body of sin might be rendered powerless.
options.note: The old man in Adam was put to death in union with Christ.

options.text: It was gradually improved until it became good.
options.note: There is real growth in holiness, but Paul speaks of a decisive crucifixion of the old self, not mere gradual improvement.

options.text: It remains fully in charge of the believer's life.
options.note: The old self was crucified so we are no longer slaves to sin.

options.text: It was left untouched by the work of Christ.
options.note: It was crucified with Him; the break is real and decisive.

quiz.q: What does Paul command believers to do in verse 11?

options.text: Count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus.
options.note: Faith reckons true what God has accomplished in Christ.

options.text: Try by willpower to make yourselves die to sin.
options.note: The believer must actively respond, but reckons a death already accomplished in Christ rather than achieving it by willpower.

options.text: Wait passively until sin stops troubling you.
options.note: Paul calls for active reckoning and then active obedience.

options.text: Assume sin no longer exists in your life at all.
options.note: Sin remains to be resisted; that is why Paul says do not let it reign.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say sin shall not be your master?

options.text: Because you are not under law but under grace.
options.note: Grace is a new reign in which sin's dominion is broken.

options.text: Because believers are now strong enough to defeat sin alone.
options.note: Believers do resist sin, but the ground is grace and union with Christ, not their own strength.

options.text: Because the law has finally made us righteous.
options.note: It is grace, not law, that frees from sin's dominion.

options.text: Because sin was never really a serious threat.
options.note: Sin is a real tyrant whose reign is broken only in Christ.

questions: Paul says believers have died to sin. How does knowing that your union with Christ is an accomplished fact change the way you fight temptation?
Baptism pictures being buried and raised with Christ. What would it mean for you to live today as someone who has truly been brought from death to life?
Paul commands us to count ourselves dead to sin and alive to God. Where do you need to reckon as true what God has already made true of you in Christ?
We are told not to present the parts of our body to sin but to God as instruments of righteousness. What specific part of your life needs to be presented to God this week?
Sin shall not be your master because you are under grace. How does living under grace, rather than under law, actually free you to pursue holiness?

translationNotes.note: How can we who died to sin renders an aorist that points to a decisive past event, not a gradual process; the believer has already died to sin's dominion in union with Christ.
Buried with Him renders synthapto, buried together with; baptism into death signifies real union with Christ's burial, sealing the end of the old life before rising to newness of life.

Count yourselves renders a word meaning reckon or consider as an accounting term; it calls for faith to treat as true the death and life already accomplished in Christ, not to pretend or to strive to make it so.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 6:1-14. No em dashes required substitution in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
